article.title: IISc Team Reports Electrons in Ultra-Pure Graphene Flow as a Near-Frictionless Dirac Fluid, Violating Wiedemann-Franz Law by Over 200-Fold
article.summary: Researchers at the Indian Institute of Science and Japan's NIMS report in Nature Physics that electrons in clean graphene decouple charge and heat transport by more than 200 times at low temperatures, forming a Dirac fluid roughly 100 times less viscous than water.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

Physicists at the Indian Institute of Science (IISc) in Bengaluru, working with collaborators at Japan's National Institute for Materials Science, have reported that electrons in ultra-clean graphene can flow as a nearly frictionless quantum liquid, decoupling charge and heat transport in ways that violate one of the oldest rules of metallic conduction. The work, published in Nature Physics and highlighted in an April 15, 2026 announcement, is described by [ScienceDaily](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2026/04/260415042152.htm) as a rare experimental realization of a long-predicted "Dirac fluid" state in a two-dimensional material.

## What We Know

The experiment targets the behavior of electrons near graphene's Dirac point, the special filling at which the material sits on the boundary between a metal and an insulator. According to [Phys.org](https://phys.org/news/2025-09-graphene-reveals-electrons-frictionless-fluid.html), the team, led by IISc PhD student Aniket Majumdar and physics professor Arindam Ghosh, measured an inverse relationship between electrical and thermal conductivity in high-purity graphene samples: as one rose, the other fell, rather than tracking together as textbook metallic theory predicts.

That behavior is a direct violation of the Wiedemann-Franz law, which for more than 150 years has tied a metal's ability to conduct heat to its ability to conduct charge through a fixed ratio. [Phys.org](https://phys.org/news/2025-09-graphene-reveals-electrons-frictionless-fluid.html) reports that the IISc group observed deviations from the law "by a factor of more than 200 at low temperatures," one of the largest breakdowns of the relation recorded in a solid-state system.

The researchers attribute the effect to the formation of a Dirac fluid, in which electrons no longer behave as independent quasiparticles but instead move collectively, much like molecules in a liquid. [AZoNano](https://www.azonano.com/news.aspx?newsID=41503) reports that the resulting fluid behaves "similarly to water, but about a hundred times less viscous," placing it close to the theoretical lower bound on viscosity and qualifying it as a nearly perfect fluid.

That low-viscosity regime is of interest well beyond condensed-matter physics. [AZoNano](https://www.azonano.com/news.aspx?newsID=41503) notes that the Dirac fluid is often compared to the quark-gluon plasma produced in high-energy particle colliders, making clean graphene a tabletop platform for studying relativistic hydrodynamics that are normally accessible only at facilities such as CERN.

## Applications and Context

The group frames the result as more than a curiosity. [ScienceDaily](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2026/04/260415042152.htm) reports that the hydrodynamic electron regime could underpin a new class of quantum sensors capable of detecting very weak electrical signals and faint magnetic fields, because charge carriers moving collectively respond to perturbations differently than conventional electron gases. [Phys.org](https://phys.org/news/2025-09-graphene-reveals-electrons-frictionless-fluid.html) similarly highlights sensing of weak electrical and magnetic signals as a plausible near-term application.

The findings extend a line of work on hydrodynamic electron flow in graphene that has been building for roughly a decade, as imaging and transport experiments have progressively shown that sufficiently pure samples can host viscous and ballistic electron fluids. The IISc result is notable for the size of the Wiedemann-Franz violation it reports and for the direct link it draws between charge-heat decoupling and the Dirac-fluid picture.

## What We Don't Know

Several practical questions remain open. Neither [ScienceDaily](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2026/04/260415042152.htm) nor [Phys.org](https://phys.org/news/2025-09-graphene-reveals-electrons-frictionless-fluid.html) specifies the exact temperature range over which the 200-fold deviation persists, the device geometry used for the thermal measurements, or how robust the effect is to disorder in less pristine graphene. It is also unclear how closely the quantitative viscosity estimate tracks the theoretical minimum set by quantum mechanics, or whether the measured transport can be reproduced in other two-dimensional Dirac materials.

The step from a laboratory demonstration in ultra-clean, low-temperature samples to a deployable quantum sensor is substantial. The IISc team has not published benchmarks against existing magnetometers or voltage sensors, and no commercial partner has been announced in the sources reviewed.

For now, the clearest takeaway is a physical one: in a material first isolated just over two decades ago, electrons can still be coaxed into collective states that behave less like a gas of particles and more like an exotic relativistic liquid, and they can do so strongly enough to break a 19th-century law of metals by more than two orders of magnitude.
